_meta.note: delta_v1 format — editorial covers what_held / what_changed / what_new. Numbers come from system_model.json + annotations. Do not manually duplicate data from those files here.

editorial.issue_title: Three Forces, One Correction, Two Revelations
editorial.context_strip: Every month the RBI publishes bank credit data across 80+ sectors. India Credit Lens builds a causal model from that data — explaining why credit moved, not just that it moved. This is Issue #2, covering February 2026 data and what the full Jan 2024–Feb 2026 series now reveals.
editorial.hero_narrative: ₹207.5L Cr in total bank credit. FY26 tracking at 13.8% — faster than all of FY25. Three structural forces are now confirmed across multiple data points. One headline number turns out to be a classification event, not growth. And connecting two months of data reveals things no single RBI file can show.

what_held.signal: Gold loans: the structural shift from unsecured to secured household borrowing
what_held.badge: ▲ Stronger than January — now confirmed across 3 data points
what_held.curr_stat: +359% over 24 months — Mar 2024, Mar 2025, Feb 2026 all confirming
what_held.note: RBI's November 2023 risk-weight increase on unsecured lending made credit cards more capital-expensive. That demand didn't disappear — it migrated to gold loans, which are secured, RBI-friendly, and competitively priced. The Jan 2026 file showed one data point at +128.8%. The merged series shows three consecutive March data points all accelerating — the structural shift is unambiguous. At ₹4.29L Cr, gold loans are still smaller than vehicle loans (₹7.21L Cr), so the runway remains.

what_held.signal: MSME credit: the formalisation wave crossing the underwriting threshold
what_held.badge: ▲ Corroborated — two independent series now agree
what_held.curr_stat: +31.2% YoY confirmed + PSL Micro & Small +25.6% FY independently corroborating
what_held.note: Five years of GST registration and UDYAM enrolment have brought millions of small businesses into formal credit data for the first time. The Jan 2026 reading of +31.2% was a single series — large enough to flag but not yet corroborated. The merged view adds the PSL Micro & Small series showing +25.6% FY growth independently. Two series, two measurement approaches, same direction. The formalisation wave has crossed the underwriting threshold at scale.

what_held.signal: PLI manufacturing capex: announced commitments becoming real bank credit drawdowns
what_held.badge: ▲ Extended — Feb data adds a third year to the arc
what_held.prev_stat: +35.9% YoY — Jan 2026, three consecutive periods accelerating
what_held.curr_stat: +62% across Feb 2024, Feb 2025, Feb 2026 — the February cross-section confirms the same trajectory
what_held.note: India's PLI scheme approved recipients in 2022-23 who entered the investment phase in FY25-26. The Jan 2026 file showed +35.9% YoY with three consecutive periods of acceleration — consistent but only visible in one cross-section. The Feb data points add a second cross-section showing the same +62% arc. This is not a January seasonal spike. PLI drawdowns are in progress, and the supply chains of anchor manufacturers are the next credit layer.

what_changed.signal: PSL Housing +38%: a number that looks like growth but isn't
what_changed.prev_read: Issue #1 flagged the +37.9% surge as suspicious — it reversed two consecutive flat periods with no visible demand catalyst. Three explanations were offered (PMAY acceleration, reclassification, genuine new origination) but left unresolved.
what_changed.curr_read: Confirmed reclassification. The Oct 2024 RBI revision raised PSL housing loan ceilings (metro: ₹35L → ₹45L, non-metro: ₹25L → ₹35L). Loans already on bank books that previously exceeded the old limits now qualify as PSL. The ₹2.87L Cr appearing as credit growth is existing exposure being relabelled — not new lending.
what_changed.implication: PSL housing achievement figures for FY26 are not comparable to prior years. Any lender or analyst citing +38% PSL housing growth as evidence of market expansion is reading a classification event as an economic signal. The affordable housing opportunity is real — PMAY, Tier 3/4 distribution gaps, strong repayment behaviour — but this particular number cannot be used to size it.

what_new.signal: FY26 is accelerating above FY25 — and you cannot see this from a single file
what_new.badge: ★ Only visible by connecting January and February data
what_new.body: Total bank credit FY26 is tracking at 13.8% (Feb 2026 vs Mar 2025) against FY25's full-year 11.0%. To compute this you need the Feb 2026 outstanding figure and the Mar 2025 base — which come from two different SIBC files. No single publication contains both. The merged series makes this comparison possible for the first time. The acceleration is not a base effect: it is consistent across both periods in the series.
what_new.implication: Any lending portfolio that grew less than 13% in FY26 to February has lost market share in an expanding system. The system is in genuine structural expansion — not post-COVID recovery, not base effect.

what_new.signal: Infrastructure credit is decelerating — and the next wave is not yet borrowing
what_new.badge: ★ Multi-period trend — not visible from any single SIBC file
what_new.stat: +5.8% FY at ₹14.44L Cr — 32.5% of all industry credit
what_new.body: Infrastructure is the largest component of industry credit and it is growing at the slowest rate in the dataset. The FY22-24 capex wave — roads, metro rail, urban utilities — is now in operations. Those projects are repaying, not drawing down. The next wave (green hydrogen, data centres, offshore wind, semiconductor fabs) has received approvals and signed PPAs, but has not yet reached credit drawdown scale. The approval-to-drawdown gap is typically 18-24 months, putting the next infrastructure credit wave at FY27-28.
what_new.implication: Corporate banks with heavy infrastructure exposure face a structural growth headwind for the next 12-18 months. The window to build credit capability for the next capex cycle — renewable energy project finance, data centre construction lending — is open now, before the drawdown wave begins.

bullets: Gold loan growth rate — does +359% sustain through Mar 2026, or do we see the first signs of saturation as early NBFC movers hit accessible TAM limits?
MSME base effect — Mar 2026 will be the first March-to-March comparison with formalisation baked into both sides. Watch whether +31% compresses as the base normalises.
PSL Housing — does the reclassification-driven +38% persist, or does growth normalise as the re-eligible loan pool is exhausted?
Infrastructure sub-sectors — first signs of the next capex wave: renewable energy project finance, data centre construction lending. Watch for any Infrastructure sub-sector breaking above +10% FY.
